question:
A 52‐year‐old man with Addison’s disease on lifelong corticosteroid replacement and a history of lateral epicondylitis presented with a 7‐day history of severe redness around his right elbow accompanied by intense burning and stinging. The redness began after he had been gardening on a cloudy summer day. Over the next days, his elbow became swollen, blisters formed and then ruptured, leaving crusted lesions. His general practitioner suspected cellulitis and prescribed dicloxacillin. Two days after starting antibiotics, he developed an itchy rash on his chest and abdomen. On examination, there was a bright red, edematous, crusted erythema over the right elbow and a maculopapular rash on the trunk. Laboratory studies, including C‐reactive protein and complete blood count, were within normal limits.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 37-year-old man presented with a 3-month history of progressive skin thickening, initially on his torso and then spreading diffusely, accompanied by a 20–30-lb weight loss and fatigue. He denied Raynaud’s phenomenon, dyspnea, or wheezing. His blood pressure at presentation was 100–110 mmHg systolic, with a serum creatinine of 0.8 mg/dL. He had a history of treated hepatitis B without active disease.

Serologic studies showed a negative antinuclear antibody, negative anti–Smith and anti–ribonucleoprotein antibodies, and low-level anti–topoisomerase I (3–4 AU/mL). Nailfold capillaroscopy was suggestive of systemic sclerosis, and a skin biopsy was read as suspicious for morphea versus systemic sclerosis. Echocardiography revealed no pulmonary hypertension or pericardial effusion. He was started on mycophenolate mofetil.

An IgG λ monoclonal protein of 1.1 g/dL was detected. Bone marrow biopsy showed 10% λ-restricted plasma cells without high-risk cytogenetics besides 1q and 5q gains, monosomy 13, and 14q deletions. Three months after presentation, for unclear reasons, he was started on high-dose prednisone (60 mg daily). Shortly thereafter, his systolic blood pressure increased to 140–150 mmHg and serum creatinine rose to 1.1 mg/dL. He developed blurry vision; ophthalmologic examination revealed cotton-wool spots. He received two intravitreal injections of bevacizumab (1.25 mg each).

One week after the injections, he was admitted with severe hypertension (systolic blood pressures 200–220 mmHg), a rise in serum creatinine to 1.4 mg/dL, and new proteinuria (urine protein-creatinine ratio 1 g/g). Renal ultrasound with Doppler showed normal-sized kidneys and no evidence of renal artery stenosis. Given the abrupt hypertension, worsening renal function, proteinuria, recent corticosteroid exposure, and intravitreal VEGF blockade, scleroderma renal crisis was suspected, and a complement-mediated thrombotic microangiopathy related to VEGF inhibition could not be ruled out. A renal biopsy was planned after blood pressure control.

What is the most likely cause of this patient's symptoms?

options.C: Chronic kidney disease with hypertensive nephropathy

question:
A previously healthy 5‐year‐old girl presented with 9 hours of intermittent, moderate–severity epigastric pain radiating to the right lower quadrant. The pain was unchanged by position and was associated with multiple episodes of nonbloody vomiting. She was afebrile, had normal urination and bowel movements, and reported a similar, self‐limited episode 1 month earlier.

On examination, she was alert, without signs of systemic infection. Abdominal palpation elicited tenderness in the epigastrium; there was no guarding or rebound. Murphy’s sign was positive, and there was no jaundice.

Laboratory studies showed normal hepatic and biliary function tests and an elevated C-reactive protein level of 30.2 mg/L. A supine abdominal radiograph was unremarkable.

Initial abdominal ultrasound demonstrated an enlarged gallbladder (54 × 34 mm) with a 3.2 mm wall thickness, pericholecystic fluid, increased pericholecystic fat, and no gallstones or intraluminal nodules. On repeat ultrasound 24 hours later, the gallbladder measured 53 × 33 mm with a 3.1 mm wall, lacked vascular flow, contained biliary sludge, and showed a cone-shaped hypoechoic structure at the neck; the fundus was displaced to the left of its fossa and moved with patient repositioning.

Contrast-enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 53.5 × 22.8 × 31.5 mm gallbladder with an irregular, poorly enhancing wall, an intraluminal hyperdense area suggestive of hemorrhage, a 3 mm hyperdense nodule, fundus deviation to the left of the gallbladder bed, pericholecystic fluid and fat stranding, and focal hepatic perfusion abnormalities.

This patient's history and findings are most suggestive of which condition?

A 51-year-old woman with Crohn’s disease on infliximab presented with a 2-day history of a bullous rash on her left arm, axilla, and lateral chest wall accompanied by subjective fever. Two days before presentation, she received her second dose of the recombinant adjuvant Shingrix vaccine. She denied new medications or topical products and had no prior similar rashes. Her Crohn’s disease was at baseline with intermittent loose stools. On examination, there was diffuse erythema and swelling from the midchest to the axilla and upper arm, with multiple bullae, some with central dusky areas; mucosal surfaces were spared. She was referred to dermatology and underwent punch biopsy; PCR testing of a bulla for herpes simplex virus types 1 and 2 and varicella zoster virus was negative.

Which of the following is the most probable diagnosis?

A 69-year-old woman with metastatic pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma (peritoneal and hepatic metastases) enrolled in a trial of SBP-101 combined with nab-paclitaxel and gemcitabine presented with a 6-month history of progressive blurred vision and visual field loss. She had no diabetes or hypertension. Baseline ophthalmologic evaluation before SBP-101 showed 20/20 visual acuity (VA) in both eyes, normal intraocular pressures, and normal macular structure on optical coherence tomography (OCT). After the second monthly cycle of SBP-101, her VA fell to 20/50 in the right eye and 20/40 in the left eye. Fundus examination revealed pigmentary changes in the macula, with punctate hypo- and hyperautofluorescent spots on blue-autofluorescence. OCT demonstrated ellipsoid zone disruption and hyperreflective material between the Bruch membrane and retinal pigment epithelium (RPE) in both eyes. SBP-101 was discontinued, and intravitreal dexamethasone was administered bilaterally without improvement. One month later, VA was 20/50 OD and 20/32 OS. Full-field electroretinography (ERG) showed normal scotopic responses and photopic amplitudes at the lower limit of normal. Serum anti-recoverin and anti-enolase autoantibodies were within normal limits. Over the next five months, VA declined to 20/5000 OD and 20/4000 OS, with photopsias and severely impaired central vision. Ultra–wide-field fundus photographs revealed bilateral roundish macular areas of irregular pigment loss extending beyond the vascular arcades, with scattered dot-blot hemorrhages. Blue-autofluorescence showed central hypoautofluorescence surrounded by a ring of alternating hyper- and hypoautofluorescent dots. OCT of the macula demonstrated incomplete RPE and outer retinal atrophy with signal hypertransmission to the choroid, ellipsoid zone disruption, and perifoveal hyperreflective sub-RPE deposits; choroidal thickness was reduced. Optic nerve OCT and RNFL thickness were normal. Visual fields showed bilateral central relative scotomas (VFI 26% OD, 31% OS). ERG per ISCEV standards revealed preserved dark-adapted 0.01 cd·s/m2 and mixed rod–cone responses, relatively preserved a-waves on light-adapted single flashes, but reduced amplitudes and slightly prolonged implicit times on 30 Hz flicker.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

A 48-year-old man of African descent was brought to the emergency department unresponsive after a generalized tonic–clonic seizure. On examination he was comatose with a blood pressure of 178/121 mmHg, heart rate 105 beats/min, respiratory rate 18 breaths/min, and a Glasgow Coma Scale score of 3 of 15; he required intubation and ICU admission. He had no history of epilepsy, head trauma, recreational drug use, smoking, or alcohol consumption. His medical history was notable for resistant hypertension diagnosed 20 years ago, treated with atenolol 50 mg, spironolactone 100 mg, amlodipine 10 mg, and valsartan 160 mg daily, with poor compliance, and for type II diabetes mellitus treated with a sulfonylurea. Family history was positive for hypertension but negative for renal disease or adrenal tumors. Body mass index was 31. Thyroid and visual-field examinations were normal, and there were no cushingoid or acromegaly features. Initial laboratory studies showed a potassium level of 2.6 mmol/L (normal 3.6–5.1), creatinine 113 μmol/L, metabolic alkalosis on blood gas analysis, urea 5.7 mmol/L, and creatine kinase 2.2 μg/L. Urinalysis was negative for leukocyte esterase and nitrites. A chest radiograph showed no signs of heart failure. ECG demonstrated sinus rhythm. Troponin-T and BNP were within normal limits. Calcium was 2.46 mmol/L. CT of the brain showed no lesions, and MRI the next day revealed no mass-occupying lesion; EEG was normal. Repeat potassium remained low at 2.6 mmol/L; plasma aldosterone was 36.2 ng/dL (normal 2.8–15.8), and plasma renin was < 0.081 ng/dL (normal 0.4–2.3). A 24-hour urine collection excluded Cushing’s syndrome and pheochromocytoma. Renal ultrasound and cardiac echocardiography excluded renovascular and cardiac causes of secondary hypertension.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 53-year-old man with no significant medical history presented with a headache. Neurologic examination revealed that he was alert and had no motor or sensory deficits, but he noted narrowing of the left lower visual field quadrant. Noncontrast CT of the head showed a 5-cm, well-circumscribed, high-density mass in the right parietal region without calcification. MRI demonstrated an extra-axial lesion that was iso‐intense on T1-weighted imaging, uniformly very low in signal on T2-weighted imaging, and showed marked enhancement after gadolinium administration. There was no diffusion restriction after correction for a T2 blackout effect. The mass was separate from the ventricles and was associated with a large anterior cyst whose contents had a signal different from cerebrospinal fluid; the cyst wall did not enhance, consistent with a peritumoral cyst, and there was surrounding edema. The tumor contacted the dura mater convexity and the falx cerebri but lacked an obvious attachment or dural tail sign. Time-resolved MR angiography (4D-TRAK) showed no tumor staining or feeding vessels, suggesting low vascularity. Routine blood tests, including tumor markers, were unremarkable. Contrast-enhanced CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis revealed no extracranial source of malignancy.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 69-year-old woman reported a 5-year history of recurrent febrile episodes every 6–8 weeks. Each episode featured a temperature of 38.5–40 °C lasting up to 7 days, accompanied by sore throat, cervical and submandibular lymphadenopathy and pain, headache, myalgia, fatigue, and dysphagia. She was asymptomatic between episodes and could predict their onset. She had a history of childhood tonsillitis treated with tonsillectomy. She worked in black metal analysis for over 30 years.  

During episodes, laboratory studies showed CRP 64 mg/L, serum amyloid A 140 mg/L, leukocytes 2.94 × 10^9/L, and neutrophils 3.19 × 10^9/L; procalcitonin, immunoglobulins, and ANA titres were normal. HIV, CMV, EBV, and other infection indicators were negative, and blood and throat cultures showed no growth. PET-CT demonstrated increased glucose metabolism in the sigmoid colon, but colonoscopy was normal. Pharyngoscopy revealed pharyngitis with white exudate. Ultrasound showed enlarged bilateral cervical, submandibular, and supraclavicular lymph nodes (up to 21 × 6 mm), and a supraclavicular lymph node biopsy revealed reactive hyperplasia. Bone marrow biopsy and cytogenetics were unremarkable. Despite multiple courses of antibiotics, febrile episodes persisted.

Which of the following best explains the patient's presentation?

A 65-year-old man was brought to the emergency department because of progressive fatigue, diffuse myalgia, and altered mental status. His medical history included chronic ischemic heart disease status post coronary-artery bypass grafting, hypertension, diabetes mellitus, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and stage 3 chronic kidney disease.  

On arrival, he reported several days of worsening leg and shoulder pain with an inability to walk independently. Vital signs showed hypotension, and an electrocardiogram revealed atrial fibrillation, a left bundle-branch block, ST-segment depression, and peaked, high T waves. Laboratory tests demonstrated a troponin T level of 532 ng/L (normal < 14 ng/L) and NT-proBNP of 5579 ng/L (normal < 125 ng/L). A temporary pacemaker was placed because of bradyarrhythmia.  

Urgent coronary angiography showed severe three-vessel atheromatous disease with an 80% stenosis in the left anterior descending artery and sub-occlusive lesions in the right coronary artery, which was successfully stented. During the procedure, he developed cardiogenic shock, treated with vasopressors and inotropic support. Arterial blood gas analysis disclosed a metabolic acidosis (pH 7.26) and a serum potassium concentration of 7.5 mmol/L.  

He was admitted to the intensive care unit. Further laboratory results included a creatine phosphokinase level > 22,000 U/L (normal 39–308 U/L), a myoglobin level > 30,000 μg/L (normal < 72 μg/L), aspartate aminotransferase 1406 U/L (normal < 50 U/L), lactate dehydrogenase 1969 U/L (normal 50–250 U/L), blood urea nitrogen 304 mg/dL (normal 10–71 mg/dL), and serum creatinine 6.58 mg/dL (normal 0.67–1.17 mg/dL).

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 32-year-old man presented with excessive tearing of the left eye and mild ocular pain. Over the previous 4–5 months he had noticed progressive left-eyelid drooping and intermittent diplopia. He reported a mild left-sided head trauma 1.5 years earlier but had no other medical history or family history of neurological or autoimmune disease.

On ophthalmologic examination, visual acuity, intraocular pressure, and funduscopy were normal. The left eyelid was ptotic, and there was limitation of adduction, elevation, and depression of the left eye, with a dilated left pupil. No other neurological deficits were found. Fluorescein angiography, indocyanine green chorioangiography, and optical coherence tomography were unremarkable. The patient noted that his ptosis fluctuated during the day, with marked improvement when washing his face with cold water.

To evaluate for myasthenia gravis, an ice pack was applied to the left eyelid for 2 minutes, after which his ptosis improved significantly, with near-complete eyelid opening; the improvement lasted only a few minutes.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

An 8-year-old girl was brought to the emergency department with reduced consciousness and an 8-day history of high fever, accompanied by vomiting, epigastric pain, and a mild cough. On arrival, her pulse was 112/min, respiratory rate 19/min, blood pressure 93/62 mm Hg, and temperature 36 °C. Physical examination was unremarkable. Neurologically, she was drowsy and disoriented (GCS 14/15), with impaired memory, visual hallucinations, and an unsteady gait; deep-tendon reflexes were normal, and plantar responses were flexor. Initial blood tests showed acute respiratory acidosis and mildly elevated liver enzymes; serum sodium was 135 mEq/L, BUN 11 mg/dL, and creatinine 0.49 mg/dL. Chest radiography revealed a slight increase in bilateral lung markings. Brain CT was normal. 

In the pediatric ICU, lumbar puncture showed CSF with protein 26 mg/dL, glucose 82 mg/dL, and 5 lymphocytes/μL. Twelve hours after admission, she developed complex partial seizures with secondary generalization and was started on phenytoin and acyclovir. By day 3, seizures increased in frequency with auditory and visual hallucinations; MRI was normal. Serologies were negative for HSV I/II, CMV, and EBV IgM, but Mycoplasma pneumoniae IgM was positive (3.44), and a 5-day course of azithromycin was begun. Blood and CSF cultures remained sterile. EEG showed diffuse slowing, rhythmic parietal–temporal discharges, and left parietal spikes. By day 4, seizures progressed to generalized myoclonic type despite levetiracetam, phenytoin, oxcarbazepine, and valproate, and her consciousness declined to GCS E2V2M5. 

On day 6, she developed marked polyuria: intake 1324 mL versus output 2606 mL, with signs of dehydration (decreased skin turgor, tachycardia, 0.6 kg weight loss). Laboratory evaluation revealed serum sodium 112 mmol/L, serum osmolality 227 mOsm/kg, urine sodium 129 mmol/L, urine osmolality 437 mOsm/kg, and a fractional excretion of sodium of 2.76×10⁻².

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

options.B: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion

question:
A 69-year-old Hispanic woman was referred for progressively worsening blurry vision, right eye greater than left. Her past ocular history included bilateral epiretinal membranes. Medical history was notable for nonspecific joint disease managed with low-dose prednisone and methotrexate, and pulmonary symptoms of unclear etiology for which she was also on low-dose prednisone. 

On examination, best-corrected visual acuity was 20/40 in the right eye and 20/25 in the left. Intraocular pressures were 13 mm Hg OU. Slit-lamp examination showed nuclear sclerotic cataracts OU (2+ OD, 1+ OS) without cell or flare. Dilated fundus examination revealed 0.5+ vitreous haze OU and mild vessel attenuation OU. Spectral-domain OCT showed preserved foveal contour without sub- or intraretinal fluid. Wide-angle fluorescein angiography demonstrated mild bilateral peripheral perivascular leakage.

Initial laboratory tests including liver function, basic metabolic panel, hemoglobin A1c, angiotensin-converting enzyme, lysozyme, QuantiFERON, syphilis serologies, anti–dsDNA, and chest radiography were within normal limits, except for a positive antinuclear antibody titer of 1:80. Complete blood count showed leukocytosis (11.6 K/µL) and anemia (hemoglobin 11.6 g/dL; previously 10.2 g/dL one year earlier).

Methotrexate was discontinued; the patient was started on mycophenolate mofetil 1000 mg twice daily and prednisone 20 mg daily. Two months later, visual acuity remained stable (20/25 OD, 20/20 OS), but repeat fluorescein angiography showed worsening peripheral perivascular leakage.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 6-year-old girl was admitted with a 5-day history of fever (to 39.5 °C) and a 2-week history of unilateral clear tearing from the right eye. She had been treated elsewhere for presumed bacterial conjunctivitis with topical ofloxacin without improvement. Over the previous 4–6 weeks she had chronic nasal obstruction, noisy breathing, and rhinolalia, and for 7 days she had purulent anterior rhinorrhea.  
On examination, she had erythematous, tender, and edematous right upper and lower eyelids with an S-shaped palpebral swelling and overlying erythema at the site of the lacrimal sac. There was painful palpation at the nasal canthus but no ocular discharge. Nasal mucosae were swollen. Bilateral cervical lymphadenopathy was present, more marked on the right. Neurologic examination, ocular motility, and funduscopy were normal, with visual acuity 20/20 bilaterally and no proptosis or relative afferent pupillary defect. Throat and abdominal examinations were unremarkable.  
Laboratory studies showed a normal white-cell count with a left shift, C-reactive protein 22 mg/L, and normal procalcitonin.  
Contrast CT of the head revealed right preseptal soft-tissue infiltration and a well-delimited ipsilateral 11 × 18 × 13 mm collection with rim enhancement suggestive of a periorbital abscess; there were no signs of acute sinusitis or intracranial extension. Multiple bilateral cervical lymph nodes were also noted.  
Intravenous co-amoxiclav (100 mg/kg/day) and topical neomycin–polymyxin B were initiated. Over the next 3 days, fever persisted, and a fluctuating lump developed in the nasolacrimal region. Systemic corticosteroids were added. No spontaneous purulent discharge occurred, and blood cultures plus eye-swab cultures remained negative. Surgical drainage was considered, and EBV serology was sent.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 47-year-old woman with HIV-1 infection presented with a 10-day history of diffuse maculopapular rash, fever, myalgias, arthralgias, choluria, pruritus, and right upper-quadrant abdominal pain. Three days before admission she developed nausea, vomiting, and decreased oral intake. Her HIV had been well controlled on zidovudine, lamivudine, and lopinavir–ritonavir for the past three years, with CD4 counts consistently >500 cells/mm3. Forty-five days before presentation her regimen was changed to zidovudine, lamivudine, and nevirapine because of gastrointestinal intolerance. Twenty days after starting nevirapine the rash and jaundice began. 

On examination: temperature 39.0°C; blood pressure 95/66 mm Hg; marked conjunctival and skin icterus; diffuse maculopapular rash covering >50% of body surface; no lymphadenopathy; abdomen soft with tenderness in the right hypochondrium; no hepatosplenomegaly; neurologic examination normal.

Laboratory studies showed: normal complete blood count and differential; normal glucose, electrolytes, and pancreatic enzymes; elevated aspartate aminotransferase, alanine aminotransferase, bilirubin, alkaline phosphatase, and γ-glutamyl transpeptidase; acute kidney injury with a threefold increase in serum creatinine and a 75% decrease in estimated glomerular filtration rate. Testing was negative for cytomegalovirus antigenemia, hepatitis A IgM, hepatitis B and C antibodies. An electrocardiogram was normal. Abdominal ultrasonography showed a liver of normal size and shape with homogeneously increased echogenicity of the parenchyma.

What is the most likely underlying etiology in this case?

A 22-year-old athletic man with no other medical history presented with two episodes of acute tetraparesis.

First episode (military service): During intense physical exercise and a stringent diet, he developed hyperventilation, hypocapnia, severe hypophosphatemia without rhabdomyolysis, and acute tetraparesis. Cerebral and spinal MRI findings were unremarkable. Workup for myasthenia gravis was negative, with normal nerve conduction studies and no acetylcholine receptor antibodies. Hypokalemic periodic paralysis was ruled out by short/long exercise tests and genetic analysis of SCN4A, KCNJ18, KCNJ2, and CACNA1S. He underwent a 4-week in-patient rehabilitation program and fully recovered within 6 months.

Second episode (one year later): He presented to the emergency department with a panic attack manifesting as acute hyperventilation and anxiety, accompanied by paresthesia and tetany in all four limbs. It was unclear whether the sensorimotor symptoms preceded or followed the panic attack. He reported normal physical activity and a balanced diet in the preceding months.

On examination, he had tetraparesis with muscle strength of 4/5 (Medical Research Council scale), a positive Trousseau’s sign on the right hand, normal cranial nerves, no sensory deficit, and normal deep tendon and plantar reflexes. Gait and balance were not assessed in the acute setting.

Arterial blood gas analysis showed acute respiratory alkalosis: pH 7.57 (NR 7.35–7.45), PaCO₂ 19.2 mmHg (NR 30–45 mmHg), PaO₂ 105 mmHg (NR 82.5–105 mmHg), HCO₃⁻ 17.7 mmol/L (NR 22–26 mmol/L), and a base excess of –1.3 mmol/L (NR ±2–3 mmol/L). Blood chemistry revealed severe hypophosphatemia (0.2 mmol/L) and rhabdomyolysis (creatine kinase 44 913 U/L; AST 397 U/L; ALT 133 U/L). Other blood tests, including sodium, potassium, magnesium, calcium, vitamins B1, B2, B6, B9, B12, amino acids, and TSH, were normal. Urinalysis showed no renal phosphate loss. Nerve conduction studies and myography were normal.

The clinical picture is most consistent with which of the following?

A 63-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of atypical right upper-quadrant abdominal pain, significant fatigue, and weight loss. Her medical history was unremarkable, with no cirrhosis, viral hepatitis, or tuberous sclerosis complex. On examination she had mild hepatomegaly without palpable mass or jaundice. Liver function tests and tumor markers (AFP, CEA) were within normal limits.  
Abdominal ultrasonography revealed a solitary, heterogeneous, hypoechoic mass in the liver with an ill-defined margin; color Doppler imaging demonstrated abundant blood flow at the lesion’s periphery. Contrast-enhanced CT showed a 6.4 × 8.0 cm low-density mass in segment IV with inhomogeneous enhancement in the portal phase. MRI confirmed a lesion that was hypointense on T1-weighted images, hyperintense on T2-weighted images, and showed strong heterogeneous enhancement after gadolinium administration. There was no lymphadenopathy, portal vein involvement, or other lesions.  
Given the hypervascular nature of the mass, its lack of adipose tissue, and absence of thick-walled vessels, the lesion was initially interpreted as a hemangioma, an atypical hepatocellular carcinoma, or a metastatic focus. Because of the patient’s worsening symptoms and indeterminate imaging findings, a central segmentectomy of the liver with cholecystectomy was performed.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 76-year-old man presented with a 5-year history of progressive leg weakness. He reported right foot slap and tripping on uneven ground, later developing bilateral foot drop, exercise-related leg fatigue, and mild difficulty climbing stairs or rising from a low chair. There was no bulbar or upper-limb involvement. He also described sensory loss in both feet to mid-shin level with mild superficial dysesthesia. A prior workup suggested bilateral L5 radiculopathy, but lumbar MRI was normal.

On examination, there was no obvious muscle atrophy. Manual strength testing revealed isolated distal lower-limb weakness: ankle dorsiflexion 2/5 on the right, 4–/5 on the left; inversion and eversion 4/5; plantar flexion difficulty when standing on toes. Hip girdle and thigh strength were 5/5. Pinprick and light-touch sensation were reduced in a length-dependent distribution; vibration and proprioception were intact. Reflexes were 1+ in the upper limbs and absent at the knees and ankles.

Laboratory studies showed normal CBC, vitamin B12, TSH, serum protein electrophoresis, hemoglobin A1c, paraneoplastic antibodies, and renal and liver function tests. Creatine kinase was mildly elevated at 508 U/L. A 30-gene Charcot–Marie–Tooth panel was negative. Because EMG showed myotonic potentials, genetic testing for myotonic dystrophy types 1 and 2 was performed and was negative.

Nerve conduction studies revealed mildly reduced peroneal and tibial CMAP amplitudes with normal distal latencies and conduction velocities. Sensory nerve conduction studies were within normal limits. Needle EMG demonstrated fibrillations, positive sharp waves, and polyphasic, long-duration motor-unit potentials with reduced recruitment in the tibialis anterior (most prominent) and gastrocnemius. Prominent myotonic discharges were also noted in several muscles. The biceps brachii and vastus lateralis were normal. Overall, these findings were interpreted as consistent with a predominantly motor length-dependent polyneuropathy.

What condition is this patient most likely suffering from?

A 62‐year‐old woman presented with angular stomatitis and intermittent mild-to-moderate abdominal pain for 6 months. She was found to be severely anemic (hemoglobin, 6 g/dL) but denied hematuria, postmenopausal bleeding, or gastrointestinal bleeding; occult blood in stool and red cells in urine were negative. Physical examination was otherwise unremarkable. Renal ultrasonography demonstrated left hydronephrosis; all other abdominal organs appeared normal. Intravenous pyelogram showed an opacity in the left renal region exerting mass effect on the pelvi‐calyceal system. Contrast‐enhanced CT of the abdomen revealed a 9.0 × 7.5 cm solid, enhancing soft‐tissue mass with focal necrosis, inseparable from the medial left kidney, displacing the kidney laterally and compressing the collecting system. At exploratory laparotomy, a radical nephrectomy specimen measured 17 × 12 × 8 cm. The cut surface showed a well‐circumscribed, lobulated, firm, gray‐white mass (10 × 9 × 8 cm) in the lower pole, with a whorled appearance, focal necrosis, and myxoid degeneration, displacing the pelvis. Microscopically, the tumor was composed of interlacing fascicles and whorls of spindle cells with moderate eosinophilic cytoplasm, oval-to‐cigar‐shaped nuclei with moderate pleomorphism, 20–25 mitoses per 10 high‐power fields, and approximately 20% of the tumor showing hemorrhage and necrosis; no epithelial component or perinephric infiltration was identified. Immunohistochemical analysis showed diffuse strong positivity for smooth muscle actin, desmin, and vimentin; epithelial membrane antigen was focally positive, while pancytokeratin and high‐molecular‐weight cytokeratin were negative.

Which diagnosis best accounts for all of the findings described?

A 16-year-old Iranian girl presented with sudden-onset bilateral horizontal gaze palsy that developed overnight. She reported paresthesia in both feet beginning 2 months earlier, progressing over 2 weeks to her trunk at the level of the umbilicus, and a similar episode in both hands about 1 year prior. She had no significant past medical history, took no medications, and denied illicit drug, tobacco, or alcohol use. On examination, she had complete bilateral horizontal gaze palsy in the horizontal plane while vertical gaze was intact aside from slightly decreased saccadic velocity. Pupils were midsize and reactive; there was no nystagmus, diplopia, or blurred vision. Mild bilateral peripheral facial nerve palsy was present. Hyperreflexia was noted in all four limbs without clonus; plantar reflexes were downgoing; muscle strength, tone, and coordination were normal. Brain MRI showed multiple hyperintense subcortical and periventricular lesions on FLAIR and T2-weighted images, and bilateral hyperintensities in the paramedian pontine reticular formation (PPRF) at the level of the pons. Spinal MRI revealed two hyperintense lesions, one at C7 and another adjacent to T4. After gadolinium, the PPRF lesions did not enhance, whereas the T4 lesion enhanced. Visual evoked potentials were normal. Serum anti–aquaporin-4 and anti–myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibodies were negative. All other laboratory tests and routine studies were unremarkable.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?

An 18-year-old woman presented with a 1-year history of slowly enlarging gingival overgrowth in the left posterior mandible that interfered with chewing but was painless. Intraoral examination revealed a 3×4 cm exophytic mass extending from the left mandibular second molar to the retromolar pad, buccally into the vestibule and inferiorly to the floor of the mouth. Panoramic radiograph showed a well-defined radiolucency around the impacted left third molar. The lesion and the impacted tooth were excised en bloc. 

Grossly, the specimen included both intraosseous and extraosseous components. Histologic examination demonstrated cords, interconnecting strands, and islands of odontogenic epithelium embedded in a cell-rich, myxoid mesenchymal stroma. The epithelial strands and cords were lined by a double layer of cuboidal cells. The islands exhibited peripheral tall columnar cells with polarized nuclei and clear, vacuolated cytoplasm surrounding central stellate reticulum-like cells. Juxtaepithelial hyalinization was noted around some islands. No hard-tissue (enamel or dentin) formation was seen. The cellularity varied, with focal hypercellular areas and other sparsely cellular, myxoid regions. A thin fibrous capsule partially surrounded the lesion. No cytologic atypia or mitotic figures were observed on multiple sections.

Based on the patient's presentation, what is the most likely diagnosis?
